# Introduction to Data Science Mathematics


Data science relies on a collection of mathematical ideas rather than a single
technique. A modern analyst needs to organize data, quantify uncertainty, fit
models, evaluate predictions, and reason about algorithms. Mathematics provides
the language for all of those tasks.

## What is data?

A dataset with $n$ observations and $p$ features can be represented by a matrix

$$
X = \begin{pmatrix}
x_{11} & x_{12} & \cdots & x_{1p}\\
x_{21} & x_{22} & \cdots & x_{2p}\\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots\\
x_{n1} & x_{n2} & \cdots & x_{np}
\end{pmatrix}.
$$

Each row corresponds to one observational unit and each column corresponds to a
measured variable.

::: {.definition}
A **feature vector** is the row vector or column vector that stores the measured
values associated with a single observational unit.
:::

## Three mathematical viewpoints

Data science can be organized around three mathematical viewpoints.

1. **Geometry**: data points live in a space such as $\mathbb{R}^p$.
2. **Uncertainty**: observations are modeled using random variables.
3. **Optimization**: models are selected by minimizing a loss function.

## Example: house-price prediction

Suppose that each house is described by size, age, number of bedrooms, and
distance to downtown. Then a row of the feature matrix may be written as

$$
x_i = (1800,\ 12,\ 3,\ 4.5).
$$

A linear model predicts the price by

$$
\hat{y}_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{i1} + \beta_2 x_{i2} + \beta_3 x_{i3} + \beta_4 x_{i4}.
$$

The coefficients $\beta_0,\ldots,\beta_4$ are unknown parameters to be learned
from data.

## Computational example

```{r}
x <- c(1800, 12, 3, 4.5)
names(x) <- c("size", "age", "bedrooms", "distance")
x
```

## Why proofs matter

Data science is computational, but computation alone is not enough. A proof
explains why a method works, when it can fail, and which assumptions are
necessary.

::: {.example}
If two analysts use the same algorithm but apply it to data with different
scales, they may obtain different answers. A mathematical analysis of the
algorithm explains why scaling matters.
:::

## Exercises

1. Give an example of a dataset that can be stored in matrix form.
2. Explain the difference between an observation and a feature.
3. Write a short paragraph describing a prediction problem and identify the
   response variable.
4. Create a $3 \times 2$ matrix in R and label its rows and columns.
